questionText: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with sides $a = BC$, $b = CA$, $c = AB$ opposite the interior angles $A$, $B$, $C$ respectively. Prove that the triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular (all three angles equal to $60^\circ$). Prove both directions, and phrase your final conclusion using necessary and sufficient conditions.

questionDiagram.description: Triangle ABC with its three sides and three interior angles labelled, used to relate equal sides to equal angles.
elements: Side a = BC, opposite angle A
Side b = CA, opposite angle B
Side c = AB, opposite angle C
Interior angles A, B, C at vertices A, B, C

steps.description: State the biconditional and the plan.
steps.explanation: The statement joins two properties with 'if and only if', so it must be proved in both directions. We prove equilateral implies equiangular first, then the converse.
The interior angles of any triangle add to $180^\circ$. This is what will pin the common angle down to a specific value once we know the angles are equal.
We start from all three sides being equal and aim to deduce the angles. The isosceles-triangle theorem links equal sides to equal opposite angles.

steps.description: Apply the isosceles theorem to one pair.
steps.workingLatex: b = c \Rightarrow B = C \quad (\text{equal sides} \Rightarrow \text{equal opposite angles})
steps.explanation: Sides $b$ and $c$ are opposite angles $B$ and $C$, so equal sides force those angles to be equal. This is the base-angles property of an isosceles triangle.

steps.description: Apply the isosceles theorem to a second pair.
steps.explanation: Repeating the argument for sides $a$ and $b$, which are opposite angles $A$ and $B$, gives another equal pair. Two overlapping equalities will chain all three angles together.

steps.description: Combine to get all angles equal.
steps.explanation: From $A=B$ and $B=C$ the three angles are all equal to one another. The triangle is therefore equiangular in the sense of having three equal angles.
Substituting three equal angles into the angle sum gives $3A = 180^\circ$. Dividing shows each angle is exactly $60^\circ$.
Equal sides have forced equal angles, each measuring $60^\circ$. This completes the first implication.
Now we begin from equal angles and must recover equal sides. The converse of the isosceles theorem provides the needed link.

steps.description: Apply the converse isosceles theorem once.
steps.workingLatex: B = C \Rightarrow b = c \quad (\text{equal angles} \Rightarrow \text{equal opposite sides})
steps.explanation: If two angles are equal then the sides opposite them are equal; this is the converse of the base-angles property, and also follows from the sine rule. So $b = c$.

steps.description: Apply the converse isosceles theorem again.
steps.explanation: Using another equal pair of angles gives a second pair of equal sides. Chaining these will force all three sides to agree.

steps.description: Combine to get all sides equal.
steps.explanation: From $a=b$ and $b=c$ all three sides are equal to one another. The triangle is therefore equilateral.
Equal angles have forced equal sides, completing the second implication. Both directions are now established.
Since each property implies the other, they are equivalent. Being equilateral is a necessary and sufficient condition for being equiangular, and vice versa.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Forward: $a=b=c$ with the isosceles theorem gives $A=B=C$, and the angle sum makes each $60^\circ$. Reverse: $A=B=C=60^\circ$ with the converse isosceles theorem gives $a=b=c$. Hence a triangle is equilateral $\iff$ it is equiangular; each condition is necessary and sufficient for the other.
